critic_raw:
Review of this radiology AI triage study:

The model demonstrates strong absolute performance (0.91 sensitivity at 0.80 specificity on 2,300 screening cases) and the technical approach — 3D convolution with prior CT inputs — is appropriate for interval growth detection.

However, the superhuman performance claim has a significant methodological weakness: the model received prior CT exams as inputs while human readers did not. For a task whose endpoint is interval nodule growth, prior exam access is the primary assessment tool. Withholding it from human readers creates an information asymmetry that likely inflates the apparent gap (0.91 vs 0.76 sensitivity). Additionally, three of four readers were general radiologists rather than thoracic specialists, which may further depress the human baseline below what would be observed in a specialist-staffed screening program.

The triage-assistance framing (not autonomous diagnosis) is appropriate and limits some of the regulatory and safety stakes. The inference-time figure (under 30 seconds) is operationally relevant and does not raise concerns.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed
The superiority claim is testable: a reader study with prior exam access and a specialist-majority panel would clarify whether the performance gap is genuine or partially artifactual.
adjudication_raw: Single-agent baseline review. Issues identified: prior access asymmetry (human_baseline_denied_priors), specialist-mix mismatch (specialist_mix_mismatch), and downstream superhuman claim overreach (superhuman_claim_overreach). The 30-second inference time and triage framing were correctly assessed as non-issues. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.

empirical_test.measure: reader sensitivity at matched specificity with prior exam access
empirical_test.success_criterion: model sensitivity remains higher than specialist-panel mean when readers have prior exam access
empirical_test.failure_criterion: specialist readers with prior access match or exceed model sensitivity, invalidating the superiority claim
